raw_case:
Intraperitoneal chronic expanding hematoma mimicking an ovarian tumor: A case report
Chronic expanding hematoma of soft tissue is a rare lesion and often misdiagnosed as neoplastic mass. It is mostly related to trauma or surgery, leading to blood-filled cysts surrounded by fibrous tissues. Symptoms may include abdominal pain, nausea or bowel obstruction. Diagnosis is often difficult, and imaging tests such as CT scans or MRIs can be inconclusive. Treatment may require complete surgical removal to prevent postoperative recurrence. We present a case of a 27-year-old woman with history of recent abdominal trauma presented with an abdominal mass, along with chronic pain and vomiting. CT scan revealed a large pelvic cystic mass, suggesting an ovarian origin which was ruled out by the MRI. Surgery was performed and histopathology revealed an intraperitoneal chronic expanding hematoma. In this case report, we review the epidemiological, clinical, and paraclinical aspects of the disease, as well as the treatment modalities.

Introduction

Chronic expanding hematoma (CEH) is a rare lesion that was first defined by Reid et al. in 1980 [1]. It refers to certain hematomas that persist as slowly expanding masses that occupy space for prolonged periods of time, ranging from months to years. It can develop after surgery, trauma, or bleeding disorders. CEH can sometimes be misdiagnosed as a malignant tumor due to its clinical and radiological features. The diagnosis of this condition is based on the patient's medical history, physical examination, and findings on various imaging modalities [1]. We present a case of a CEH of the peritoneal cavity that was suspected on imaging and confirmed by histopathological examination.

Case report

We report the case of a 27-year-old woman, with a history of pulmonary tuberculosis and recent abdominal trauma with lumbar fracture, who presented with chronic pain and nausea. Physical examination revealed a palpable abdominal mass. Biology showed mild anemia, and tumor marker tests were normal.A CT scan was performed and showed an abdomino-pelvic cystic mass, that measure 183 × 170 × 73 mm, with hypodense content. Other organs, such as the liver, spleen and pancreas showed no abnormalities. An ovarian origin was suggested.An MRI was performed, which showed a well-defined peritoneal cystic mass with hyperintensity on T1- and T2-weighted images, with few areas of low T2 signal intensity and no fatty component. The mass also had a peripheral capsule with dark signal on T2-weighted images. The large mass was compressing adjacent organs without evidence of invasion. The ovarian origin was ruled out by the visualization of 2 multifollicular ovaries independent of the mass. A small amount of peritoneal effusion was also observed (Fig. 1).The diagnosis of chronic expanding hematoma was therefore retained, given the notion of previous trauma.We performed a laparotomy through a midline incision. Upon exploration, a large abdomino-pelvic mass was found, adherent to the anterior wall, the right colon, transverse colon, left colon and small bowel. Surgical excision of the mass was performed with free tumor margin and without any further organ resection (Fig. 2). Drainage was performed at the end of the surgery. The postoperative course was uneventful, and the patient was discharged on postoperative day 3.The excised cyst was sent to the pathology department for histopathological analysis. Upon gross examination, the mass measured 18 × 13 × 14 cm. At sectioning, the cyst had a thin wall with a friable, necrotic-hemorrhagic content (Fig. 3). Histologically, the wall consisted of hyalinized, hypocellular fibrous tissue, associated with a chronic inflammatory infiltrate and newly formed capillaries. It was devoid of any epithelial lining. The cavity contained necrotic debris, fibrin, and blood clots. Extensive sampling was conducted to exclude the presence of any neoplastic component. On the basis of these findings, diagnosis of an expanding hematoma was maintained (Fig. 4).

Discussion

Chronic expanding hematoma of soft tissue is a rare entity characterized by its persistence and increasing size after the initial hemorrhage. Most hematomas grow to a certain extent; they are compressed by the surrounding tissues and organs, and are then naturally absorbed. However, some hematomas expand gradually over a period of time and are called chronic expanding hematomas. They are most often reported in soft tissues, such as extremities or the thoracic cavity, but remain rare in the abdominal cavity [2,3].While mesenteric injuries after trauma are rare, they hold significant clinical importance due to their potential associations with intraperitoneal, gastrointestinal, or retroperitoneal bleeding, as well as intestinal ischemia, stenosis, or perforation [4].This condition is most frequently encountered in patients between the age of 20 and 80 years, although cases beyond this age range have been reported [2].The exact mechanism responsible for chronic expanding hematoma remains unclear, although it is most commonly seen as the result of various types of injuries, such as surgical interventions, seat belt syndrome, blunt trauma, and it is rarely spontaneous [2,5].It has been described that the initial trauma results in a displacement of the skin and the subcutaneous fatty tissue deeply away from the fascia and underlying muscle leading to the formation of blood- filled cysts surrounded by a dense fibrous tissue. The self-expanding nature of the hematoma appears to be due to the irritant effect of blood and its breakdown products leading to repeated exudation or bleeding from capillaries in the granulation tissue of the cyst. The nutrient vessel supplying the mass may have been the source of the initial hematoma [2,6,7].The most frequent clinical symptom is abdominal pain, the severity of which depends on the location and size of hematoma. Nausea, constipation or bowel obstruction may occur if the hematoma is large enough to compress the digestive tract. Nonspecific symptoms make it difficult to confirm the diagnosis of chronic mesenteric hematoma, it is usually identified through history and after conventional imaging techniques, even though, it is frequently misdiagnosed as a neoplastic mass [4,5].The diagnosis of CEH has been made using a variety of imaging modalities. It has been reported that in these cases, dynamic CT scanning can identify a rim enhancement in the arterial phase because the hematoma capsule contains granulation tissue with vascular channels [8]. Although MRI is inferior to CT in identifying calcifications or spatial resolution, MRI is more sensitive than CT in diagnosing hematomas. The signal within the lesion on MRI can change over time, indicating temporal changes in hemoglobin levels. High signal intensity on T1-weighted images is due to the presence of methemoglobin within the hematoma. Some soft tissue tumors, like lipomas, liposarcomas, and hemangiomas, can also exhibit increased signal intensity on T1-weighted images. However, distinguishing hematomas from malignant soft tissue tumors based on clinical and radiological findings can be challenging due to the time-dependent alterations in MRI signals [9].According to Liu et al., it is important to consider CEH as a potential diagnosis when encountering soft tissue masses that show internal hemorrhage and a fibrous pseudocapsule on unenhanced T1- and T2-weighted MRI. If the contrast enhancement within the lesion is uneven or patchy, the possibility of hemorrhagic sarcomas should be taken into account [10]. In our case, the predominant observation was a high signal intensity on both T1- and T2-weighted images, except for a few regions with low T2 signal intensity. The difference in the signal is probably due to different stages of the hematomas [8]. A mixed signal in a CEH has been called the mosaic sign [11] . These atypical MRI findings indicated the presence of CEH [12].Accurate diagnosis of chronic expanding hematoma can be established through histopathological examination[13].Pathologically, organized hematoma consists of a mixture of dilated vessels, hemorrhage, fibrin exudation, fibrosis, hyalinization, and neovascularization [3].The outer layer is characterized by dense fibrous tissue containing hemosiderin deposits and clusters of macrophages. The intermediate layer exhibits rough connective tissue, while the inner layer comprises granulation tissue featuring neovascular capillaries [13].When treating chronic expanding hematoma, it is difficult to differentiate by preoperative imaging if it is a malignant tumor. CT/MRI findings lack specificity regarding chronic expanding hematoma [3].Aspiration of the material or cyst content may not be effective because of the fibrous wall that is left. Complete surgical excision, including removal of the pseudo capsule, is the optimal treatment for chronic expanding hematoma, since aspiration of the fluid content or incomplete excision could lead either to an unconfirmed diagnosis or recurrence [2].In cases involving the peritoneal or retroperitoneal cavity, combined resection of adjacent organs may be necessary, especially if the lesion is large [3].

Conclusion

In conclusion, CEH is a rare condition that can be misdiagnosed as a malignant tumor due to its clinical and radiological features. Accurate diagnosis of CEH can be challenging, even with imaging techniques such as CT and MRI. Surgical excision with complete removal of the pseudocapsule is the optimal treatment to prevent recurrence. Histopathological examination plays a crucial role in confirming the diagnosis of CEH. Although rare, CEH should be considered in the differential diagnosis of patients presenting with chronic pain, abdominal mass, and a history of trauma or surgery.

Guarantor of submission

The corresponding author is the guarantor of submission.

Ethical approval

Our institution does not require ethical approval for reporting individual cases or case series.

Patient consent

Written informed consent for the publication of this case report was obtained from the patient.

generate_case.case_summary:
- **Patient Information:** 27-year-old woman
- **Chief Complaint:** Chronic pain, nausea, and vomiting
- **History of Present Illness:** Recent abdominal trauma with lumbar fracture; presence of a palpable abdominal mass
- **Past Medical History:** History of pulmonary tuberculosis
- **Personal History:** None
- **Family History:** None
- **Physical Examination:** Palpable abdominal mass
- **Ancillary Tests:**
  - Laboratory tests: Mild anemia, normal tumor marker levels
  - CT scan: Large abdomino-pelvic cystic mass measuring 183 × 170 × 73 mm with hypodense content; no abnormalities in liver, spleen, pancreas
  - MRI: Well-defined peritoneal cystic mass with hyperintensity on T1- and T2-weighted images and dark peripheral capsule on T2; normal multifollicular ovaries ruled out ovarian origin; small peritoneal effusion observed
  - Histopathology of excised mass: Cyst with thin wall and necrotic-hemorrhagic content, characterized by hyalinized hypocellular fibrous tissue, chronic inflammatory infiltrate, capillaries, necrotic debris, and blood clots without epithelial lining or neoplastic component
generate_case.differential_diagnosis:
1. The initial differential diagnosis included a neoplastic ovarian mass based on the patient's symptoms, physical exam, and CT imaging findings that suggested an ovarian origin.
   - **Rationale for Considering:** The large abdominopelvic cystic mass and associated symptoms such as chronic pain, nausea, and vomiting are characteristic of ovarian cystic lesions.
   - **Exclusion:** MRI findings excluded an ovarian origin by identifying independent multifollicular ovaries and by showing features of a fibrous pseudocapsule around the mass.
   
2. Other potential causes of abdominopelvic masses were considered, such as retroperitoneal tumors, mesenteric cysts, or hemorrhagic sarcomas.
   - **Rationale for Considering:** The large size, persistence, and compressive effects of the mass raised suspicion of a malignant or benign neoplastic source.
   - **Exclusion:** Mixed signal on MRI (mosaic sign) and histopathological findings (no epithelial lining, neoplastic features, or malignancy) ruled out neoplastic origins.

3. The chronic expanding hematoma was considered due to the patient's abdominal trauma history and imaging characteristics, including a fibrous pseudocapsule and varied MRI signals suggesting different hematoma stages.
   - **Confirmation:** Histopathological examination of the surgical specimen confirmed chronic expanding hematoma, with characteristic findings of fibrous tissue, chronic inflammation, and vascular granulation tissue.
generate_case.final_diagnosis: Intraperitoneal chronic expanding hematoma secondary to recent abdominal trauma. The diagnosis was confirmed by imaging findings (CT and MRI) that demonstrated a well-defined cystic mass, and by histopathological analysis that excluded malignancy and established hallmark features of a chronic expanding hematoma.

results.content:
### Reasoning:
<step 1> **Analyze Chief Complaint and History of Present Illness:** The patient presents with chronic pain, nausea, and vomiting, suggestive of an abdominal pathology.  The history of recent abdominal trauma with a lumbar fracture is highly significant, especially in the context of a palpable abdominal mass. This strongly suggests a potential complication related to the trauma.

<step 2> **Evaluate Physical Examination and Ancillary Tests (Imaging):** The palpable abdominal mass is confirmed on physical examination and further characterized by CT and MRI as a large abdomino-pelvic cystic mass. The imaging descriptions (hypodense content on CT, T1 and T2 hyperintensity with dark capsule on MRI) are suggestive of a fluid-filled lesion, possibly containing blood products given the T1 hyperintensity and history of trauma.  The location being peritoneal and ovarian origin ruled out are important details.

<step 3> **Consider Past Medical History and Other Histories:**  The past history of pulmonary tuberculosis is less directly relevant to the acute abdominal presentation, but it's noted. Personal and family history are unremarkable.

<step 4> **Interpret Laboratory Findings:** Mild anemia is present, which is non-specific but could be related to chronic inflammation, blood loss associated with trauma, or the mass itself. Normal tumor markers are crucial in reducing the likelihood of a primary malignant cystic tumor.

<step 5> **Focus on Histopathology - The Definitive Clue:** The histopathology report is the most critical piece of information. The description of a cyst with a thin wall, necrotic-hemorrhagic content, hyalinized fibrous tissue, chronic inflammatory infiltrate, capillaries, necrotic debris, blood clots, and *absence of epithelial lining or neoplastic component* is highly characteristic. The lack of epithelial lining is key in differentiating it from true cysts (which have epithelial lining). The necrotic-hemorrhagic content and fibrous tissue are consistent with an organized hematoma or a pseudocyst formation.

<step 6> **Synthesize all findings to formulate a diagnosis:**  Combining the history of abdominal trauma, the imaging findings of a cystic mass with features suggestive of blood products, and the histopathology report confirming a cyst without epithelial lining and containing hemorrhagic and necrotic debris within a fibrous wall, the most likely diagnosis is a **post-traumatic peritoneal pseudocyst** resulting from the organization of a hematoma.  The absence of epithelial lining and neoplastic components rules out epithelial cysts and tumors. The trauma history strongly supports a post-traumatic etiology.

<step 7> **Differential Diagnosis and Exclusion:** Consider other cystic abdominal masses, but the history of trauma and histopathology findings are most consistent with a post-traumatic pseudocyst.  Ovarian cysts are ruled out by MRI and histopathology. Mesenteric cysts or lymphangiomas are less likely given the trauma history and histopathological description. Abscess is less likely given the chronic nature and histopathology lacking acute inflammatory features (though chronic infiltrate is present, consistent with organization of hematoma). Neoplastic cysts are ruled out by histopathology and normal tumor markers.

<step 8> **Final Diagnostic Conclusion:** Based on the comprehensive analysis, the final diagnosis is a post-traumatic peritoneal pseudocyst.

### Answer:
Post-traumatic peritoneal pseudocyst
